questions.prompt: Choose the correct passive form: "The windows ___ every week." (present simple passive)
questions.explanation: The passive = "be" + past participle. "The windows" is plural and the time is present (every week), so we use ARE + cleaned: the windows are cleaned. "clean" is active; "is cleaned" wrongly uses the singular "is"; "were cleaned" is past, not present.

questions.prompt: Turn this active sentence into passive: "Someone broke the vase." The correct passive is:
questions.explanation: The active verb "broke" is past simple, so the passive keeps the past tense: "the vase" (singular) + WAS + past participle "broken". "is broken" changes the tense to present; "were broken" wrongly uses the plural "were"; "was break" uses the base verb instead of the past participle.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Arabic ___ in Tunisia." (present simple passive of 'speak')
questions.explanation: "Arabic" (a language) is singular, so the present simple passive uses IS + the past participle "spoken": Arabic is spoken in Tunisia. "speaks" is active; "are spoken" wrongly uses the plural "are"; "was spoken" is past, not present.

options.text: The letters were written by the students.
The letters were write by the students.
The letters was written by the students.
The letters are written by the students yesterday.

questions.explanation: "The letters" is plural and the action is past, so we need WERE + past participle (written): The letters were written by the students.

questions.prompt: Why is the passive preferred in: "Oil was discovered in the desert"?

options.text: Because the subject is performing the action.
Because the action is happening right now.
Because the agent is unimportant; the focus is on the discovery.

questions.explanation: We use the passive here because the agent (who discovered it) is unimportant or unknown — the focus is on the result: oil being discovered.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The exam ___ yesterday by all the students." (past simple passive of 'take')
questions.explanation: "yesterday" signals past tense and "the exam" is singular, so the past simple passive is WAS + the past participle "taken": the exam was taken. "is taken"/"are taken" are present; "took" is the active past, not the passive.
